rationale.R1.2: The results report $124/yr revenue and $187/yr degradation cost under RegRank (net –$63/yr), yet the conclusion asserts 'positive net returns to participating households.' This is a direct arithmetic contradiction in the core argument; any improvement task that leaves it standing fails.
rationale.R1.3: The conclusion claim of net positive returns is load-bearing for the paper's case, and Table 2's own figures refute it. Evidence and claim are misaligned at the paper's climactic inference.
rationale.R1.6: 'Improve this draft' canonically includes prose quality as a central expected responsibility; sentence-level and paragraph-level revision is a baseline expectation of the delegation.
rationale.R1.7: The draft asserts all simulated vehicles use LFP chemistry, then cites Peterson et al. (2010) — who explicitly characterised NMC cycling losses — and adopts their NMC per-cycle capacity-loss coefficients for the LFP model. This chemistry mismatch undermines the degradation analysis and must be addressed in any substantive improvement.

parsed.clarification_question: Should improvements be limited to prose style and readability, or should they also cover technical content — specifically whether the numerical results, cited evidence, and degradation model assumptions actually support the stated conclusions?
